claims.claim_text: Incident-style SaaS anecdote claims EKS costs rose from $38,000 to $61,000 during a quiet reliability week.
claims.anchor_text: A platform team at a growing SaaS company had a quiet reliability week and a very loud finance meeting. Their EKS clusters had no active incidents, the on-call rotation was calm, and customer-facing latency looked normal. The monthly cloud bill still climbed from $38,000 to $61,000, and nobody could explain which product, team, or workload caused the increase.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A platform team at a growing SaaS company had a quiet reliability week and a difficult finance meeting. Their Kubernetes clusters had no active incidents, the on-call rotation was calm, and customer-facing latency looked normal. The monthly cloud bill still rose sharply, and nobody could explain which product, team, or workload caused the increase.
claims.rationale: The anecdote contains fake-specific spend figures and a vendor setting without a named, citable postmortem.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes scheduling uses resource requests, not live usage, when deciding whether Pods fit on nodes.
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes resource management documentation states that the scheduler checks summed container requests against node capacity and can reject placement even when actual usage is low.

claims.claim_text: FinOps showback reports costs without internal billing, while chargeback assigns actual internal costs to teams or business units.
claims.rationale: The FinOps Foundation page is the primary source for showback and chargeback definitions, but finops.org is not on the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: OpenCost provides Kubernetes and cloud spend visibility with allocation by Kubernetes concepts such as namespace, controller, pod, and related resource dimensions.
claims.rationale: The OpenCost README describes Kubernetes workload and cloud cost monitoring, resource allocation, and allocation queries.

claims.claim_text: OpenCost uses Kubernetes object data, Prometheus metrics, and pricing inputs or integrations to calculate allocation and cost signals.
claims.rationale: The official OpenCost installation documentation directly covers the Prometheus requirement and setup, but opencost.io is not on the allowlist.

claims.claim_text: OpenCost is installed and managed via its Helm chart, including the opencost Helm repository used in the module.
claims.rationale: The upstream README states that OpenCost is installed via the official Helm chart and shows the opencost Helm repository.

claims.claim_text: OpenCost's allocation API supports windowed allocation queries and aggregation by namespace, controller, and labels such as label:team.
claims.rationale: The OpenCost API documentation directly lists window and aggregate parameters including namespace, controller, pod, and label aggregation, but opencost.io is not allowlisted.

claims.claim_text: OpenCost can expose a UI on port 9090 and an API on port 9003 through kubectl port-forward.
claims.rationale: The OpenCost API docs use localhost:9003 for the API and document port-forwarding that also exposes the UI on 9090, but the host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Exposing cost data publicly can reveal namespace names, team labels, workload names, and internal architecture, so access control should govern it.
claims.rationale: Kubernetes RBAC documentation provides the authoritative access-control model for controlling who can read cluster resources and operational data.

claims.claim_text: Deployment metadata labels and Pod template labels are different places; running Pods receive labels from the Pod template.
claims.rationale: The Deployment documentation describes the Pod template and selector/label relationship used for Pods managed by a Deployment.

claims.claim_text: Admission policy can require workload labels such as team, environment, and cost-center before objects enter the cluster.
claims.rationale: The ValidatingAdmissionPolicy documentation includes label-requirement examples and explains admission-time validation.

claims.claim_text: Request reductions create potential savings, but actual cloud-bill savings require fewer or smaller provisioned nodes or avoided future scale-outs.
claims.rationale: Kubernetes node autoscaling documentation explains provisioning and consolidation around scheduling capacity rather than billing changes from workload requests alone.

claims.claim_text: CPU pressure commonly appears as throttling or latency, while memory pressure can terminate containers with OOMKills.
claims.rationale: Kubernetes resource management documentation discusses CPU throttling under limits and memory enforcement through OOM termination.

claims.claim_text: Spot capacity can cost much less than on-demand capacity, but the provider can interrupt it.
claims.rationale: The AWS EC2 Spot page directly describes discounted spare capacity and interruption characteristics.

claims.claim_text: Scaler service accounts should be limited to the target namespace and the specific resources they change, following least privilege.
claims.rationale: Kubernetes RBAC documentation defines namespace-scoped Roles and resource/verb-specific permissions for least-privilege access.

claims.claim_text: ResourceQuotas can cap aggregate requested CPU, memory, object counts, PVCs, pods, and LoadBalancer Services in a namespace.
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes ResourceQuota documentation lists compute quotas and object-count quotas including services.loadbalancers and persistentvolumeclaims.

claims.claim_text: LimitRanges can provide default requests and limits and enforce minimum and maximum resource values for containers in a namespace.
claims.rationale: The LimitRange documentation directly describes default, minimum, and maximum resource constraints for namespace objects.

claims.claim_text: A Deployment object may be created even when ResourceQuota prevents all of its Pods from being admitted, because Pod creation is rejected as quota is exceeded.
claims.rationale: The ResourceQuota documentation states that workload objects may be created while managed Pods fail to exist when quota is exhausted.

claims.claim_text: Deleting a PVC can delete underlying storage depending on the PV or StorageClass reclaim policy.
claims.rationale: The PersistentVolume documentation explains Delete and Retain reclaim policies and how Delete can remove the associated external storage asset.

claims.claim_text: A Kubernetes Service of type LoadBalancer can provision an external cloud load balancer.
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes Service documentation describes type LoadBalancer and cloud-provider load balancer provisioning.

claims.claim_text: A precise team cost of $12,834.19 is used as an illustrative report value.
claims.anchor_text: A report that claims a team owes `$12,834.19` may look authoritative while resting on assumptions about idle allocation, shared daemon overhead, or cloud discounts.
claims.suggested_rewrite: For instance, a report that claims a team owes an exact dollar amount may look authoritative while still resting on assumptions about idle allocation, shared daemon overhead, or cloud discounts.
claims.rationale: The exact dollar amount is a teaching number, not a citable historical price or actual bill.

claims.claim_text: A 70% CPU request reduction is used as a precise illustrative risk comparison.
claims.anchor_text: A `70%` CPU request reduction on a critical checkout service may be riskier than deleting a confirmed idle load-test namespace.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A large CPU request reduction on a critical checkout service may be riskier than deleting a confirmed idle load-test namespace.
claims.rationale: The percentage is illustrative and uncited; statistic-class claims should remove the precise number rather than soften it.

claims.claim_text: OpenCost is currently described as a CNCF Sandbox project that originated from Kubecost.
claims.anchor_text: OpenCost is a CNCF Sandbox project that originated from Kubecost and focuses on Kubernetes-native cost allocation rather than invoice-only cloud reporting.
claims.suggested_rewrite: OpenCost is a CNCF project focused on Kubernetes-native cost allocation rather than invoice-only cloud reporting.
claims.rationale: CNCF announced OpenCost's move to Incubating status in 2024, so the current Sandbox wording is stale.

further_reading.title: OpenCost README
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary upstream project reference for OpenCost capabilities, Helm installation, and Kubernetes cost allocation scope.

further_reading.title: OpenCost Advances to CNCF Incubating Status
further_reading.why_relevant: Provides current CNCF project status and historical context for OpenCost's origins.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes Resource Management for Pods and Containers
further_reading.why_relevant: Authoritative Kubernetes reference for requests, limits, scheduler fit, CPU throttling, and memory OOM behavior.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes Resource Quotas
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers namespace quotas, object-count limits, and the enforcement behavior used in the lab.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes Node Autoscaling
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains how resource requests affect node provisioning and consolidation decisions.

notes: Worked examples, quiz prompts, and hands-on exercise values were not cited unless they stated a reusable product, Kubernetes, pricing, or security behavior outside the local exercise.
